authors: Nghi D. Q. Bui
title: Building Effective AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
storage_format: ACE Playbook (bullets of learned strategies, scored by effectiveness + recency + semantic scoring, selected for injection into the system prompt) + Session Storage (full JSON transcripts with metadata, persisted per session for continuation) + Snapshot Manager (tracking of file operations via git for per-step undo).
llm_used: Model-agnostic (supports Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Alibaba, etc. via per-workflow model binding with 5 model roles: action, thinking, critique, vision, compact)
benchmarks_used: NR (mentions Terminal-Bench and LongCLI-Bench but does not formally evaluate)
performance_metrics: NR
baseline_compared: NR (qualitatively compares with Claude Code, Goose, OpenCode, Crush, Aider, SWE-Agent, OpenHands)
memory_size_over_time: Playbook grows with learnings; context compaction in 5 progressive stages (70%→99% utilization); prompt caching reduces input token cost by ~88%
token_reduction: ~88% input token reduction via prompt caching (Anthropic); context compaction in 5 stages (warning→masking→pruning→aggressive masking→full LLM summarization)
main_result: Technical report describing OpenDev, an open-source terminal-native agent in Rust with the ACE memory system (Playbook + Sessions), adaptive context compaction in 5 stages, 5 defense-in-depth security layers, and a multi-model architecture with per-workflow binding. No formal empirical evaluation.
performance_vs_baseline: NR
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: No empirical evaluation on benchmarks; no quantitative comparison with other agents; the paper is a descriptive technical report; effectiveness of the ACE memory system not experimentally validated; single author
validity_threats: Lack of empirical evaluation makes claims unverifiable; comparisons with proprietary systems (Claude Code) based on external observation; design decisions justified by development experience but without ablations; generalization to different workflows untested

authors: Silin Chen, Shaoxin Lin, Yuling Shi, Heng Lian, Xiaodong Gu, Longfei Yun, Dong Chen, Lin Cao, Jiyang Liu, Nu Xia, Qianxiang Wang
title: SWE-Exp: Experience-Driven Software Issue Resolution
storage_format: Multi-facet Experience Bank in a vector database (Multilingual-E5-Large embeddings): each experience is a dictionary with issue_type (descriptive label, e.g. VariableReferenceError), description (conditions and scenarios), and perspective or modification (abstract insights). Comprehension experiences guide diagnosis (problem perspective); modification experiences guide safe code editing (repair strategies). Offline extraction via the Experiencer agent from successful and failed trajectories.
benchmarks_used: SWE-Bench Verified (500)
performance_metrics: Pass@1 (%), Average Token Costs, Average API Costs ($), Average Wall Time
baseline_compared: Agentless, SWE-Agent, SWE-Search, OpenHands, EvoCoder, SAGE
memory_size_over_time: Fixed after offline construction of the experience bank; gain saturation at ~300 experiences (Figure 4b); performance plateaus with 1-2pp variation beyond that point
main_result: SWE-Exp achieves 73.0% Pass@1 with Claude 4 Sonnet and 42.0% with DeepSeek-V3-0324 on SWE-bench Verified, surpassing all baselines. Comprehension and modification experiences contribute independently (+3.2% and +2.6%); experience extraction is the most critical component (-6.0% without it).
performance_vs_baseline: +2.2pp vs SWE-Search with Claude 4 Sonnet (73.0% vs 70.8%); +6.6pp vs SWE-Search with DeepSeek (42.0% vs 35.4%); +3.2pp vs SWE-Agent with DeepSeek (42.0% vs 38.8%); +4.2pp vs EvoCoder with DeepSeek (42.0% vs 38.0%)
cost_vs_baseline: $0.13 vs $0.12 per issue with DeepSeek (+8.3%); wall time 15min49s vs 12min37s (+25%); retrieval+rerank overhead 37.5s avg
limitations: Effectiveness depends on the quality of extracted experiences; k>1 experiences degrade performance (42.0%→39.0% with k=4); evaluation only on Python projects; the Instructor may become over-dependent on experiences even when direct exploration would suffice
validity_threats: Single model per configuration (DeepSeek or Claude 4 Sonnet); SWE-bench may overlap with the LLM's training data; temporal leakage mitigated but residual (excludes same-repo + post-target); evaluation only on Python; experience quality assessed on a subset of 75 instances

authors: Yi-Xuan Deng, Xiaoqin Liu, Yi Zhang, Guo-Wei Yang, Shuojin Yang
title: Your Code Agent Can Grow Alongside You with Structured Memory
storage_format: Vector database (FAISS) of memory entries structured as sextuples: (commit message o, code changes c, functional keywords k, problem description p, root-cause analysis r, summarized solution s). Built from historical commits via an LLM polisher (Defect Management theory). New entries are added via self-internalization of human-verified solutions.
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500)
performance_metrics: Resolved rate (%), number of resolved issues
baseline_compared: OpenHands (Claude Opus 4.5, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3 Pro), TRAE, DaubanCode, Live-SWE-agent, EPAM AI/Run, Atlassian Rover, ACoder, Warp
memory_size_over_time: Grows monotonically with repository commits + internalized solutions; top-k retrieval controls exposure
main_result: MemCoder achieves 78.8% (pass@1) and 83.8% (pass@2) with GPT-5.2 on SWE-bench Verified, comparable to SOTA. With DeepSeek-V3.2, it reaches 77.8% vs 68.4% without memory (+9.4pp), showing that structured commit memory unlocks general-purpose models for complex SE.
performance_vs_baseline: +9.4pp vs no memory with DeepSeek-V3.2 (77.8% vs 68.4%); +3.4pp vs OpenHands+GPT-5.2 (78.8% vs ~74.4%); top-6 on the SWE-bench Verified leaderboard
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Evaluation only on SWE-bench Verified; depends on a rich commit history; memory quality limited by the LLM's distillation capability; retrieval overhead not quantified; ablation on a 200-instance subset
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified as the only benchmark; DeepSeek-V3.2 and GPT-5.2 as the only models; memory built from commits of the same repository being evaluated (temporal leakage controlled but residual); cross-encoder reranker may introduce unreported latency

authors: Darshan Deshpande, Varun Gangal, Hersh Mehta, Anand Kannappan, Rebecca Qian, Peng Wang
title: MemTrack: Evaluating Long-Term Memory and State Tracking in Multi-Platform Dynamic Agent Environments
venue: NeurIPS 2025 Workshop: Scaling Environments for Agents (SEA)
storage_format: NA — benchmark agnostic to the backend; evaluates Mem0 (ChromaDB, embeddings) and Zep (temporal knowledge graph) as memory components
agent_framework: NA — evaluates agents with GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro + multi-platform tools (Slack, Linear, Git)
llm_used: GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o-mini (embeddings for Mem0), GPT-4o (LLM-judge)
benchmarks_used: MemTrack (47 curated instances, multi-platform: Slack, Linear, Git in a dockerized environment)
performance_metrics: Correctness (LLM-judge), Efficiency (tool call count), Redundancy (tool call overlap), tool call entropy
baseline_compared: LLM+NoMem, LLM+Mem0, LLM+Zep (for GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro)
memory_size_over_time: NA
main_result: Frontier LLMs such as GPT-5 reach only ~60% Correctness on MemTrack; memory components (Mem0, Zep) do not significantly improve performance and increase tool call redundancy (~21%). Performance degrades on follow-up questions.
performance_vs_baseline: GPT-5+Mem0 0.610 vs GPT-5+NoMem 0.601 (+1.5%); Gemini+Mem0 0.118 vs Gemini+NoMem 0.144 (-18%); Zep with no significant gain
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Dataset limited to 47 instances; only two LLMs evaluated; Mem0 and Zep used with default configurations without optimization; simulated environment may not capture the full complexity of real workflows; no latency or cost evaluation
validity_threats: Only GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro tested; Mem0 and Zep with default configurations; LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o) for Correctness may introduce bias; simulated scenarios vs real enterprise workflows; 5 runs for statistical significance on a small dataset

authors: Kris Shengjun Dong, Sahil Modi, Dima Nikiforov, Sana Damani, Edward Lin, Siva Kumar Sastry Hari, Christos Kozyrakis
title: KernelBlaster: Continual Cross-Task CUDA Optimization via Memory-Augmented In-Context Reinforcement Learning
storage_format: Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base (~50KB JSON): hierarchy of performance states (memory-bound, compute-bound, hybrid) → lists of candidate optimizations with expected performance scores. Each entry ⟨state, (optimization, score)⟩. Updated via PolicyEvaluation (compares result vs prediction), PerfGapAnalysis (analyzes discrepancy), ParameterUpdate (rewrites the KB).
benchmarks_used: KernelBench Level 1, Level 2, Level 3 (NVIDIA A6000, H100, L40S GPUs)
performance_metrics: Geometric mean speedup, fast_p (% kernels with ≥r× speedup), ValidRate
baseline_compared: AI CUDA Engineer, IREE compiler, PyTorch (Eager + torch.compile), Kernelsseum (zero-shot), no_mem_agent (ablation)
memory_size_over_time: ~50KB Knowledge Base; grows with discovered states (~5.5 average states per kernel); accumulated optimizations ~60 unique after 8000 attempts
token_reduction: 2.4× fewer tokens than the minimal agent without a Knowledge Base
main_result: KernelBlaster achieves geometric mean speedups of 1.43×, 2.50× and 1.50× on KernelBench Level 1-3 vs PyTorch. The persistent Knowledge Base enables cross-task and cross-GPU transfer, with a 1.67× gain over the memoryless agent.
performance_vs_baseline: GeoMean 1.43× (L1), 2.50× (L2), 1.50× (L3) vs PyTorch; 1.67× vs no_mem_agent; surpasses AI CUDA Engineer in fast_p for moderate to high speedups
cost_vs_baseline: 2.4× fewer tokens than the minimal agent without KB; KB reusable across GPUs (~50KB compact)
limitations: Evaluation limited to NVIDIA CUDA kernels; Level 3 has scaling challenges with single optimization per iteration; the Knowledge Base may be biased toward early entries; dependence on detailed NCU profiling for quality feedback
validity_threats: KernelBench as the only benchmark; specific NVIDIA GPUs (Ampere, Hopper, Ada); reward hacking possible even with correctness verification; comparison with AI CUDA Engineer under different evaluation conditions (models, hardware, test harness)

authors: Pengfei Du
title: Memory for Autonomous LLM Agents: Mechanisms, Evaluation, and Emerging Frontiers
storage_format: NA — survey that proposes a three-dimensional taxonomy (temporal scope, representational substrate, control policy) and classifies 5 families of mechanisms: in-context compression, retrieval stores, reflective memory, hierarchical/virtual memory, and policy-learned memory
agent_framework: NA — survey covers RAG, Reflexion, Generative Agents, Voyager, MemGPT, MemoryBank, ExpeL, ChatDB, AgeMem, among others
benchmarks_used: NA — analyzes LoCoMo, MemBench, MemoryAgentBench, MemoryArena as memory evaluation benchmarks
performance_metrics: NA — proposes a 4-layer stack: task effectiveness, memory quality, efficiency, governance
baseline_compared: NA
memory_size_over_time: NA
main_result: Survey that formalizes agent memory as a write-manage-read loop coupled to the POMDP cycle. Proposes a taxonomy with 3 dimensions (temporal scope, representational substrate, control policy) and identifies consolidation, causal retrieval, reliable reflection, and learned forgetting as the most critical open challenges.
performance_vs_baseline: NA
cost_vs_baseline: NA
limitations: No formal search/selection methodology for the survey; no original empirical data; coverage may be incomplete; comparative analysis based on qualitative descriptions of the systems
validity_threats: Article selection without a reproducible protocol; proposed taxonomy not empirically validated; conclusions about gaps based on qualitative observation of the field; single-author survey without formal peer review (preprint)

authors: Yaoqi Guo, Ying Xiao, Jie M. Zhang, Mark Harman, Yiling Lou, Yang Liu, Zhenpeng Chen
title: EET: Experience-Driven Early Termination for Cost-Efficient Software Engineering Agents
storage_format: Structured experience objects (JSON: issue_id, task_description, execution_summary, evaluation_result, confidence, confidence_reason); abstractions of successful resolution trajectories stored in an experience base
agent_framework: custom (EET — agnostic plugin evaluated on top of Agentless, Mini-SWE-Agent, Trae Agent)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500 tasks; 207 from SWE-bench Lite for experience generation)
performance_metrics: % Resolved, # API Calls, # Input Tokens, # Output Tokens, Total Cost ($)
baseline_compared: Agents without EET (Agentless, Mini-SWE-Agent, Trae Agent), Turn-control (Gao and Peng 2026), Naive-EET (RAG with raw trajectories)
input_tokens_per_task: Varies by agent: Agentless ~72K/task, Mini-SWE ~176K/task, Trae ~3.6M/task (without EET); average reduction of 29.9% with EET
output_tokens_per_task: Varies by agent: Agentless ~3.7K/task, Mini-SWE ~7.6K/task, Trae ~46.4K/task (without EET); average reduction of 25.1% with EET
memory_size_over_time: Experiences generated from 207 SWE-bench Lite tasks; base fixed for evaluation (does not grow during testing)
cost_per_task: Agentless GPT-5-mini: $0.028→$0.012/task (-55.1%); Trae GPT-5-mini: $0.324→$0.232/task (-28.2%); average -31.8% across 6 configurations
token_reduction: -29.9% input tokens, -25.1% output tokens, -20.8% API calls on average
main_result: EET reduces total cost by 19.3-55.1% (average 31.8%) across 3 agents × 2 LLMs, with negligible loss in resolve rate (maximum 0.2%). Early termination identifies opportunities in 11.3% of issues on average.
performance_vs_baseline: +2.7% average resolve rate vs agents without EET; Agentless benefits most (+7.5% abs with GPT-5-mini)
cost_vs_baseline: -31.8% average total cost vs without EET; vs Turn-control: -31.8% vs -41.4% (Turn-control reduces cost more but degrades resolve rate by -10.7%)
limitations: Evaluation limited to SWE-bench Verified; reliance on historical data for cold start in new domains; cross-agent transferability untested; experiences are specific to each agent
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified as the only benchmark; GPT-5-mini and DeepSeek-V3.2 as the only models; experiences generated from SWE-bench Lite may not represent real production issues; threshold tuning on the same dataset may overestimate gains

authors: Md Tanzib Hosain, Salman Rahman, Md Kishor Morol, Md Rizwan Parvez
title: Xolver: Multi-Agent Reasoning with Holistic Experience Learning Just Like an Olympiad Team
storage_format: Dual memory: (1) Episodic memory D_E — external corpus of past problems with reasoning traces and solutions (OpenMathReason for math, cp-algorithms C++ for code), retrievable via BM25 or parametric LLM self-retrieval; updated with each solved problem (Xolver+). (2) Intermediate shared memory D_S — fixed-size dynamic buffer (|D_S|=m) holding the top-m tuples (reasoning trace T, response R, feedback (T_S, s), agent a) ranked by the Judge agent's score s; evolves at each intra-problem iteration.
benchmarks_used: GSM8K, AIME 2024 (30 problems), AIME 2025 (30 problems), Math-500, LiveCodeBench v5
performance_metrics: Accuracy (%), Pass@1
baseline_compared: LongCoT (direct backbone), Self-Reflexion, OctoTools, Search-o1, CheatSheet, CodeSim; proprietary models (o1, o3, o4-mini-high, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash); open-weight (DeepSeek-R1, Qwen3-235B); specialized (rStar-Math, AlphaOne, OpenMathReason, OpenCodeReason)
input_tokens_per_task: ~45-135k total tokens per problem (input+think+output combined, varies by dataset and backbone; Figure 8)
memory_size_over_time: D_E grows monotonically with Xolver(+) cross-problem updates; D_S is a fixed-size per-problem buffer of size m (resets between problems); average gain of +3.5pp from dynamic episodic memory
token_reduction: NR (~1.5× tokens vs Search-o1 single-agent, but with substantial performance gains)
main_result: Xolver with o3-mini-medium reaches 97.1% GSM8K, 93.8% AIME'24, 89.4% AIME'25, 99.2% Math-500 and 87.3% LiveCodeBench, consistently surpassing all baselines including specialized frameworks. With o3-mini-high it sets new SOTA: 98.1% GSM8K, 94.4% AIME'24, 93.7% AIME'25, 99.8% Math-500, 91.6% LiveCodeBench. Cross-problem episodic memory contributes +3.5pp on average.
performance_vs_baseline: +18.6pp AIME'24 avg vs o3-mini-medium LongCoT (93.8% vs 75.8%); +13.6pp AIME'25 (89.4% vs 75.8%); +21.0pp LiveCodeBench (87.3% vs 66.3%); surpasses o4-mini-high on AIME'24 (93.8% vs 93.4%) and LiveCodeBench (87.3% vs 69.5%)
cost_vs_baseline: ~1.5× tokens vs Search-o1; update complexity O(mI) vs O(n²) of CheatSheet; runtime parallelizable across agents
limitations: High computational cost (~1.5× tokens of single-agent); dependent on backbone LLM quality; external retrieval corpus in C++ for Python tasks (cross-language); self-judging inferior to external judging (-9.9% code, -3.88% math); focus on math and competitive programming, with no evaluation on real SE
validity_threats: Math and competitive programming benchmarks may not generalize to real SE; 16-32 runs for variance but no formal confidence intervals; curated retrieval corpus (OpenMathReason, cp-algorithms) may not represent retrieval quality in other domains; comparison with proprietary models uses officially reported results

authors: Thomas Joshi, Shayan Chowdhury, Fatih Uysal
title: SWE-Bench-CL: Continual Learning for Coding Agents
storage_format: FAISS vector index storing embeddings of task-attempt summaries (problem, solution, rationale, tool use, success status). Embeddings: OpenAI text-embedding-3-small or nomic-embed-text. Top-k retrieved and prepended to the prompt.
agent_framework: swe_agent (LangGraph-based, inspired by SWE-agent, with ACI: Navigation/Search, File Viewing, Editing, Execution)
benchmarks_used: SWE-Bench-CL (273 tasks, 8 Python repositories, chronologically ordered with curriculum learning)
performance_metrics: ACC, F (forgetting), FT (forward transfer), BWT (backward transfer), AULC, TUE (tool-use efficiency), CL-Score, CL-F_β, Pass rate
continual_learning_metrics: ACC, F, FT, BWT, AULC, TUE, CL-Score, CL-F_β (this paper DEFINES the CL metrics for coding agents)
baseline_compared: memory-enabled vs memory-disabled agents (proposed protocol); standard SWE-bench harness (preliminary)
memory_size_over_time: Grows linearly with processed tasks; no compaction or forgetting mechanism
main_result: Proposes SWE-Bench-CL, the first continual learning benchmark for coding agents (273 tasks, 8 repos). Defines a CL metric suite (ACC, F, FT, BWT, AULC, TUE, CL-F_β). Preliminary analysis reveals low inter-task similarity (Jaccard 0.11, cosine 0.18) and high sensitivity to prompt poisoning. The SWE-bench harness is incompatible with CL evaluation.
performance_vs_baseline: Preliminary, inconclusive: pass rates <8.5% with and without memory; the memory-enabled condition does not improve (garbage-in, garbage-out from harness incompatibility)
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Only 8 Python repositories with up to 50 issues; difficulty estimated by human fix time (subjective); static sequence with curriculum ordering does not reflect real dynamics; full experiments are proposed but not carried out
validity_threats: Benchmark limited to Python; artificial curriculum ordering; proposed CL metrics not yet empirically validated at scale; SWE-bench harness incompatible with SWE-Bench-CL; preliminary models are relatively weak (7-13B)

authors: Peiran Li, Jiashuo Sun, Fangzhou Lin, Shuo Xing, Tianfu Fu, Suofei Feng, Chaoqun Ni, Zhengzhong Tu
title: Traversal-as-Policy: Log-Distilled Gated Behavior Trees as Externalized, Verifiable Policies for Safe, Robust, and Efficient Agents
storage_format: Gated Behavior Tree (GBT): single-rooted executable tree with 21 task-family roots. Each macro node = (NL description, coarse tags, risk_level, behavior_signature σ = (σ_disc, σ_cont), env_context pointer). Deterministic gates: RuleGates (code predicates over structured ctx) + ContentGates (frozen Llama-Guard-3-8B classifier, temp=0). Spine memory = compact sequence of visited macros as long-term state. Self-evolution (GBT-SE): local repair via analogical successes with regression testing preserving safety invariants.
llm_used: GPT-4o (primary base model), Llama-3-8B, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking (executor decoupling); Llama-Guard-3-8B (ContentGate classifier)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500), WebArena (812), GPQA (448), AgentSafetyBench, AgentHarm (public), Agent Security Bench (ASB)
performance_metrics: Success Rate (SR%), Coverage (Cov%), Violation Rate (Viol%), Unsafe Success (USucc%), Tok/Chars (thousands), AgentSafetyBench score, HarmScore, ASB metrics
baseline_compared: OpenHands CodeActAgent (gpt-4o native), +Global guardrail only, +GBT-Basic, +GBT-SE (progressive ablation ladder)
memory_size_over_time: GBT grows via self-evolution (new children, gates); spine memory is compact per-episode (visited macros only); the tree is a persistent cross-benchmark artifact
token_reduction: -39% Tok (208k→126k) and -40% Chars (820k→490k) on SWE-bench Verified; -45% Tok (94k→52k) and -43% Chars (360k→205k) on WebArena
main_result: GBT-SE raises success rate from 34.6% to 73.6% on SWE-bench Verified and from 19.7% to 66.9% on WebArena, while reducing violations to 0.2% and unsafe success to 0.0%. Tokens reduced ~40%. With 8B executors (Qwen3-VL-8B), GBT more than triples success on SWE-bench (14.0%→58.8%), demonstrating decoupling between offline reasoning capacity and online execution.
performance_vs_baseline: +39.0pp SWE-bench (73.6% vs 34.6%); +47.2pp WebArena (66.9% vs 19.7%); +28.5pp GPQA (87.3% vs 58.7%); violations 2.8%→0.2% SWE-bench; unsafe success 1.2%→0.0%
cost_vs_baseline: Tok/Chars 208k/820k→126k/490k SWE-bench (-39%/-40%); 94k/360k→52k/205k WebArena (-45%/-43%); 8B executors viable with the same GBT artifact
limitations: Explicit coverage boundary: outside coverage (covered=0), GBT only guarantees primitive-level safety; log distillation may inherit biases from OpenHands trajectories; 21 fixed task families may not cover all domains; gates depend on structured ctx that may omit relevant state
validity_threats: GPT-4o as primary base model; GBT distilled from OpenHands logs may not transfer to other frameworks; deterministic safety gates over ctx may fail if ctx omits relevant fields; Protocol A evaluation (cross-benchmark distillation) may have residual semantic overlap; macro abstraction stability tested but not guaranteed for all scenarios

authors: Tobias Lindenbauer, Georg Groh, Hinrich Schütze
title: From Knowledge to Noise: CTIM-Rover and the Pitfalls of Episodic Memory in Software Engineering Agents
storage_format: CTIM items (textual rules distilled from successful trajectories via Knowledge Distillation with o1; general + repository-level) + exemplar trajectories (full trajectory text) stored in a Milvus index (Code-T5 embeddings)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (subset: 401 train, 45 test)
performance_metrics: Success rate (% instances resolved)
baseline_compared: AutoCodeRover (no CTIM), ablation configurations (CTIM only, General CTIM only, Repo-level CTIM only, Exemplar only)
memory_size_over_time: Bounded by sqrt(n) constraint; CTIM grows sublinearly with the number of available trajectories
main_result: AutoCodeRover 42% vs CTIM-Rover 40% (best config); CTIM only degrades to 31%. Episodic memory introduces noise that degrades performance; noisy CTIM items steer the agent away from productive exploration.
performance_vs_baseline: -2pp vs baseline (40% vs 42%); CTIM only -11pp (31% vs 42%)
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Small test sample (45 instances); ~10% of samples removed due to environment issues; KD limited to trajectory pairs; CTIM not filtered by relevance to the problem; embedding-based retrieval not investigated as a remedy for noise
validity_threats: SWE-bench subset may not be representative; AutoCodeRover as the sole framework; unbalanced repository distribution (django dominates train and test); GPT-4o may not be ideal for knowledge distillation

authors: Alireza Nadafian, Alireza Mohammadshahi, Majid Yazdani
title: KAPSO: A Knowledge-grounded framework for Autonomous Program Synthesis and Optimization
storage_format: Knowledge system: MediaWiki typed pages (Principle, Implementation, Environment, Heuristic) with a Neo4j graph index + Weaviate vector index, ingesting repos, docs, papers and web content from 2000+ sources. Cognitive memory: episodic store in a Weaviate vector DB with JSON fallback (trigger, lesson, recommended action, provenance for the experiment branch). Experimentation engine: isolated git branches with committed artifacts.
agent_framework: custom (KAPSO: OrchestratorAgent + CodingAgent + SearchStrategy + ContextManager + KnowledgeSearch)
benchmarks_used: MLE-Bench (Kaggle-style ML competitions), ALE-Bench (AtCoder heuristic contests, C++)
performance_metrics: Medal rate (%), Private score, Final Performance (ELO), Rank Percentile (%), Total Cost ($)
baseline_compared: R&D-Agent, AIRA-dojo, ML-Master, AIDE (MLE-Bench); ALE-Agent, ALE Sequential, ALE one-shot (ALE-Bench)
memory_size_over_time: Knowledge base is fixed (pre-built); episodic memory grows with executed experiments; CTIM items bounded by configurable capacity
cost_per_task: ALE-Bench: $914.8 total (11 competitions) ≈ $83/competition; MLE-Bench: $200/task budget, 24h runtime
main_result: MLE-Bench: 50.67% medal rate (vs 35.11% best open-source R&D-Agent, +15.56pp); ALE-Bench: ELO 1909.4, rank percentile 6.1% (vs ALE-Agent 1879.3, 6.8%) at 9% lower cost ($914.8 vs $1003.3).
performance_vs_baseline: +15.56pp medal rate vs R&D-Agent on MLE-Bench; +30.1 ELO vs ALE-Agent on ALE-Bench; advantage concentrated on Hard tasks (+17.78pp)
cost_vs_baseline: ALE-Bench: $914.8 vs $1003.3 ALE-Agent (-8.8%); MLE-Bench: fixed $200 budget does not allow direct comparison
limitations: Limited number of competitions in ALE-Bench introduces noise; variance across tasks; reliance on proprietary LLMs (Gemini); high cost per task; the knowledge package may have selection bias
validity_threats: MLE-Bench and ALE-Bench are ML/algorithm competitions, not traditional SE (bug fixing, code localization); Gemini-2.5-pro as the sole model; knowledge base built by the authors with potentially subjective selection criteria

authors: Chen Qian, Jiahao Li, Yufan Dang, Wei Liu, YiFei Wang, Zihao Xie, Weize Chen, Cheng Yang, Yingli Zhang, Zhiyuan Liu, Maosong Sun
title: Iterative Experience Refinement of Software-Developing Agents
storage_format: Pool of shortcut experiences as key-value pairs: solution-to-instruction (instructive agent's knowledge) and instruction-to-solution (responsive agent's knowledge). Shortcuts are extracted from non-adjacent nodes in the execution chain. Embeddings via text-embedding-ada-002 for vector retrieval.
benchmarks_used: SRDD dataset (1200 software requirement descriptions, 40 categories, split into 6 batches)
performance_metrics: Completeness (α), Executability (β), Consistency (γ), Quality (α×β×γ), Duration
baseline_compared: GPTEngineer, MetaGPT, ChatDev, ECL (Experiential Co-Learning)
memory_size_over_time: Grows with batches; heuristic elimination shrinks it to 11.54% of the original pool (930 of 8053 experiences) while preserving superior performance
main_result: IER improves Quality from 0.4665 (ChatDev) to 0.6372 (IER-Successive), +36.6% relative. The cumulative pattern offers greater stability than successive; experience elimination reaches superior performance with only 11.54% of the original pool.
performance_vs_baseline: +36.6% Quality vs ChatDev (0.6372 vs 0.4665); +10.3% Quality vs ECL (0.6372 vs 0.5775); +11% Executability vs ECL (0.9146 vs 0.8643)
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Evaluation only with ChatGPT-3.5; custom metrics may not reflect real software quality; SRDD limited to simple software generation; no evaluation on standard benchmarks (SWE-bench); duration increases with IER (~180s vs ~148s ChatDev)
validity_threats: ChatGPT-3.5 as the sole model; Completeness/Executability/Consistency are automated proxies without human validation; SRDD generates simple software unrepresentative of real projects; ada-002 embeddings may not adequately capture semantic similarity of code

authors: Ming-Tung Shen, Yuh-Jzer Joung
title: TALM: Dynamic Tree-Structured Multi-Agent Framework with Long-Term Memory for Scalable Code Generation
storage_format: Vector database with entries containing: task description, reasoning process, generated code, and tree depth metadata. Automatic consolidation: entries with similarity > 0.95 are merged by the LLM into a unified representative entry (shared problem descriptions + solution patterns + code); originals are removed to prevent uncontrolled growth. Retrieval: top-3 ANN search filtered by tree depth.
benchmarks_used: HumanEval (164), BigCodeBench (1140), ClassEval (100)
performance_metrics: Pass@1
baseline_compared: Direct Prompting, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Planning Prompting, Reflexion, MapCoder
input_tokens_per_task: NR (significantly lower than MapCoder per Figure 4)
memory_size_over_time: Grows with solved tasks; merge consolidation reduces redundancy (entries with sim > 0.95 are fused); gains emerge after ~30 tasks (Figure 5)
token_reduction: ~50% fewer tokens than MapCoder on ClassEval (Figure 4); marginal overhead with memory enabled vs without
main_result: TALM with memory reaches 92.55% HumanEval, 53.27% BigCodeBench and 38.00% ClassEval (class level) with GPT-4o, surpassing all baselines. Memory contributes most on complex tasks (BigCodeBench +2.14pp, ClassEval class +2.00pp). Structured tree decomposition with localized re-reasoning complements experience accumulation.
performance_vs_baseline: +3.17pp BigCodeBench vs Direct (53.27% vs 50.10% GPT-4o); +5.00pp ClassEval class vs Direct (38.00% vs 33.00%); +2.00pp ClassEval class vs MapCoder (38.00% vs 36.00%); ~50% fewer tokens than MapCoder
cost_vs_baseline: ~50% fewer total tokens than MapCoder on ClassEval; marginal memory overhead vs TALM without memory
limitations: Relatively small datasets (HumanEval 164, ClassEval 100); memory reset at each experimental round; only GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o tested; only function/class-level generation, with no evaluation on real repositories (SWE-bench); fixed hyperparameters
validity_threats: Only OpenAI models (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o); HumanEval/ClassEval are function/class-level, not representative of real SE; memory reset between rounds does not reflect continuous accumulation; comparison with re-implemented MapCoder may differ from the original; ClassEval with 100 tasks may have high variance

authors: Kangning Shen, Jingyuan Zhang, Chenxi Sun, Wencong Zeng, Yang Yue
title: Structurally Aligned Subtask-Level Memory for Software Engineering Agents
storage_format: Memory state S_sub as a repository of structured subtask entries: each entry m = (z, d, e) where z = functional category (ANALYZE, REPRODUCE, EDIT, VERIFY), d = structured description (objective + mechanism keywords), e = transferable abstract experience extracted by the LLM Extractor. Indexed by embeddings; updated online after each subtask completion.
llm_used: Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 4.0 Sonnet
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500)
performance_metrics: Pass@1 (Avg ± std over 3 runs, Best@3)
baseline_compared: Vanilla Agent (Mini SWE Agent without memory), Instance-level Memory (ReasoningBank reproduction)
memory_size_over_time: Grows monotonically during the streaming protocol (initialized empty); 797 unique entries after 500 instances; long-tail distribution (top-100 = 33.8% of retrievals, 50.7% single-use)
main_result: Subtask-level memory improves Pass@1 by +4.7pp on average over vanilla agents across 4 LLMs on SWE-bench Verified. Gains are larger on Hard tasks (+8.7pp, >28 steps) and grow with accumulated experiences. Category isolation is critical: global retrieval loses +2.3pp.
performance_vs_baseline: +6.8pp Gemini 2.5 Pro (60.3% vs 53.5%); +5.6pp Gemini 2.5 Flash (41.9% vs 36.3%); +3.9pp Claude 3.7 Sonnet (56.1% vs 52.2%); +2.3pp Claude 4.0 Sonnet (65.8% vs 63.5%); average +4.7pp
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Evaluation only on SWE-bench Verified; memory reset every experimental round (does not test true long-term accumulation); experience extraction by the same LLM backbone may limit quality; top-1 retrieval may miss relevant experiences
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified is predominantly Python; memory reset undermines long-term scalability evaluation; 4 models tested but only Google and Anthropic families; Instance-level Memory is a reproduction of ReasoningBank, not the original implementation; variance across runs (σ up to 2.27)

authors: Saksham Sahai Srivastava, Haoyu He
title: MemoryGraft: Persistent Compromise of LLM Agents via Poisoned Experience Retrieval
storage_format: NA — security paper analyzing vulnerabilities in LLM agent memory stores. Attacks MetaGPT DataInterpreter with a poisoned RAG store (BM25 + FAISS vector index) containing 100 benign experiences + 10 poisoned ones.
agent_framework: NA — evaluates an attack against MetaGPT DataInterpreter
benchmarks_used: Custom evaluation set (12 handcrafted queries for MetaGPT DataInterpreter, 110 experience seeds)
performance_metrics: Poisoned Retrieval Proportion (PRP), qualitative behavioral drift
baseline_compared: MetaGPT DataInterpreter without poisoned memory, BM25-only vs BM25+FAISS union retrieval
memory_size_over_time: NA
main_result: MemoryGraft demonstrates that 10 poisoned experiences out of 110 total (9.1%) achieve a Poisoned Retrieval Proportion of 47.9%, persistently compromising agent behavior. The attack persists across sessions, is trigger-free, and exploits the semantic imitation heuristic.
performance_vs_baseline: PRP 47.9% with union retrieval (BM25+FAISS) vs ~25% with BM25-only; 23 out of 48 total retrievals return poisoned experiences
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Small-scale evaluation (110 seeds, 12 queries); only MetaGPT DataInterpreter as target; only GPT-4o; attack design is semi-white-box (attacker knows which artifacts the agent ingests); no quantitative behavioral severity metrics; proposed defenses not empirically evaluated
validity_threats: Only one agent framework (MetaGPT); GPT-4o as the sole LLM; 12 handcrafted evaluation queries; attack scenario assumes the agent runs README code without verification; small scale may not represent real deployments

authors: Hongjin Su, Ruoxi Sun, Jinsung Yoon, Pengcheng Yin, Tao Yu, Sercan Ö. Arık
title: Learn-by-interact: A Data-Centric Framework for Self-Adaptive Agents in Realistic Environments
storage_format: Pool of synthesized examples (~10k after filtering): instruction-trajectory pairs generated via self-instruct + backward construction from environment documentation. Filtered through deduplication + an LLM committee. Stored as text for retrieval in ICL or fine-tuning.
llm_used: Claude-3.5-sonnet, Gemini-1.5-pro (generation and evaluation); Codegemma-7B, Codestral-22B (fine-tuning)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified, WebArena, OSWorld, Spider2-V
performance_metrics: Resolve rate (%), success rate (%)
baseline_compared: Baseline (vanilla prediction), RAG, Data distill, Reflexion, LATS
memory_size_over_time: Pool fixed after offline synthesis (~10k examples per benchmark); no growth during inference
token_reduction: Fewer LLM calls and tokens than Reflexion (~15 calls vs ~25) and LATS (~15 calls vs ~35) with superior performance
main_result: Learn-by-interact improves the baseline by up to +10.1pp (Claude, OSWorld: 12.4%→22.5%) and +8.8pp (Claude, SWE-bench: 51.2%→60.0%) via ICL with agentic retrieval. Fine-tuning with synthesized data raises Codestral-22B from 4.7% to 24.2% on WebArena (+19.5pp).
performance_vs_baseline: +8.8pp SWE-bench (Claude), +10.1pp OSWorld (Claude), +5.4pp SWE-bench (Gemini); surpasses Reflexion and LATS across all 4 benchmarks with both LLMs
cost_vs_baseline: Fewer LLM calls and tokens than Reflexion and LATS during inference; synthesis cost is offline and amortized
limitations: High data synthesis cost (many LLM calls for generation and filtering); dependence on existing environment documentation/resources to generate instructions; resources may be incomplete or unavailable
validity_threats: Claude-3.5-sonnet used to both generate and evaluate data may bias results; documentation may be incomplete for specific environments; backward construction assumes sub-trajectories are independent; ICL evaluation with models that generated the synthesis data

authors: Valentin Tablan, Scott Taylor, Gabriel Hurtado, Kristoffer Bernhem, Anders Uhrenholt, Gabriele Farei, Karo Moilanen
title: Smarter Together: Creating Agentic Communities of Practice through Shared Experiential Learning
storage_format: Knowledge base: ~34000 documentation blobs from data science libraries (NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, SciPy, scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow) indexed as embeddings + text. Experiential memory: structured traces of agent-user interactions (problem context, recommendation, feedback), clustered by semantic similarity and curated into generalizable patterns. Continual learning via curation epochs.
agent_framework: custom (Spark memory layer, integrated via MCP)
benchmarks_used: DS-1000 (1000 data science problems from StackOverflow: NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, SciPy, scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow)
performance_metrics: Code Quality Score (1-5, LLM judge: Gemini 2.5 Pro), Helpfulness Score (1-5, LLM judge: Claude Sonnet 3.7)
baseline_compared: NO-SPARK (LLMs without external memory), DS-1000 Human Reference solutions (4.28/5 average)
memory_size_over_time: Initialized with ~34K documentation blobs; grows via experiential learning epochs (1 epoch evaluated in this study)
main_result: Spark improves code quality across all models: Qwen3-Coder +0.66 (4.23→4.89), Haiku 4.5 +0.41 (4.50→4.91), GPT-5-Codex +0.05 (4.78→4.83). The 30B open-weights model with Spark matches GPT-5-Codex without Spark. 98.2% of recommendations rated at least GOOD.
performance_vs_baseline: +0.66 code quality Qwen3-Coder (4.23→4.89); +0.41 Haiku 4.5 (4.50→4.91); +0.05 GPT-5-Codex (4.78→4.83); all WITH-SPARK exceed human quality (4.28)
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Synthetic experiential data (generated by GPT-4o, not real interactions); LLM-judge evaluation without execution metrics (Pass@1); DS-1000 as the sole benchmark (data science); only 1 experiential learning epoch evaluated
validity_threats: LLM-as-judge may correlate with the evaluated models; synthetic experiences do not represent real interactions; DS-1000 focuses on data science rather than general SE (bug fixing, repository-level); stronger models benefit less (ceiling effect); Spark is a commercial product, potential bias

authors: Aristidis Vasilopoulos
title: Codified Context: Infrastructure for AI Agents in a Complex Codebase
storage_format: Tier 1: Markdown constitution (~660 lines, always loaded; conventions, checklists, trigger tables, failure modes). Tier 2: 19 Markdown agent specifications (115-1233 lines each, ~9300 total; domain knowledge, code patterns, symptom-cause-fix tables). Tier 3: 34 Markdown knowledge base documents (~16250 lines; subsystem specs). Total: ~26200 lines (24.2% of the C# code). Retrieval via MCP server (keyword matching).
benchmarks_used: NA (observational case study: 108K lines of C#, 283 sessions, 70 days part-time)
performance_metrics: Sessions (283), agent invocations (1197), agent turns (16522), MCP retrieval calls (1478 over 218 sessions), knowledge-to-code ratio (24.2%)
baseline_compared: Implicit: before vs after introducing each infrastructure component (observational, no control)
memory_size_over_time: Growth documented over 70 days: Phase 1 (~100 lines constitution), Phase 2 (specs + agents), Phase 3 (MCP retrieval). Final: ~26200 lines across 54 files. Knowledge-to-code ratio stabilized at ~24%
main_result: A 3-tier codified context infrastructure supported the construction of a 108K-line distributed system by a solo AI-assisted developer in 70 days. 4 case studies: cross-session coordination (74 sessions without save bugs), captured experience reused across 10+ sessions, documentation as a persistent investment, debugging with embedded domain knowledge.
performance_vs_baseline: Observational: save system spec referenced across 74 sessions without bugs; UI sync spec reused across 10+ sessions; knowledge base consulted 1478 times across 218 sessions
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: Single developer on a single project; specific domain (real-time distributed simulation) demands more documentation than typical projects; observational methodology without control; implementation specific to Claude Code + MCP
validity_threats: Single developer, single project, single tool (Claude Code); observational methodology without randomization; confounding with the developer's growing experience; game/simulation domain may not represent typical SE; spec staleness caused at least 2 incidents

authors: Boshi Wang, Weijian Xu, Yunsheng Li, Mei Gao, Yujia Xie, Huan Sun, Dongdong Chen
title: Improving Code Localization with Repository Memory
storage_format: Corpus of recent commits (episodic: messages + patches + linked issues, searchable via BM25 with an LLM tokenizer) + functionality summaries of active files (semantic: NL text generated by LLM for the top-200 most-edited files)
agent_framework: LocAgent (localization framework based on a heterogeneous graph)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench-verified (500 examples, 12 repositories), SWE-bench-live (130 examples, 62 repositories)
performance_metrics: Accuracy@1, Accuracy@3, Accuracy@5
baseline_compared: CodeRankEmbed, Agentless, LocAgent (without memory)
memory_size_over_time: 7000 commits + 200 most-edited files per repository; size fixed by design (fixed window of recent commits)
cost_per_task: $0.58-$0.89/example (RepoMem) vs $0.54-$0.59/example (LocAgent); higher cost concentrated on hard problems where LocAgent fails
main_result: Acc@5 76.5% on SWE-bench-verified (+4.9% vs LocAgent); episodic and semantic memory contribute in a complementary manner; the agent shifts from brute-force exploration to hypothesis-driven investigation
performance_vs_baseline: +4.9% absolute Acc@5 vs LocAgent on SWE-bench-verified; +3.1% on SWE-bench-live; resolve rate +3.4% end-to-end (40.4% vs 37.0%)
cost_vs_baseline: Higher cost on hard problems ($0.54→$0.89 when LocAgent fails); high per-example variance; memory is a strategic investment on hard problems
limitations: Performance degrades on repositories with limited history (<10K commits); variable, problem-dependent cost; retrieval method not optimized for code vocabulary; explicit links between commits and issues may be noisy or absent
validity_threats: Evaluation only on bug fixing/localization; GPT-4o as the sole model; pre-issue commits may have variable quality; the fixed 7K-commit window may not be ideal for every repository

authors: Peiding Wang, Li Zhang, Fang Liu, Chongyang Tao, Yinghao Zhu
title: CodeMEM: AST-Guided Adaptive Memory for Repository-Level Iterative Code Generation
storage_format: AST key-value pairs (Code Context Memory: code blocks indexed by function/class signature with internal and external APIs) + code-centric session blocks with AST diffs (Code Session Memory: prompt, code, modification note, AST diff per round)
benchmarks_used: CodeIF-Bench (L-2), CoderEval (extended multi-turn)
performance_metrics: IA (Instruction Accuracy), CA (Conversation Accuracy), IFR (Instruction Forgetting Rate), Pass@1
baseline_compared: Full-Context BM25, Full-Context RL-Coder, MemGPT, Mem0, A-Mem
memory_size_over_time: Filtered by the AST Selector each round; growth controlled by API intersection
token_reduction: Reduction of ~30k tokens vs multi-turn dialogue methods on CodeIF-Bench (107.8k vs 131.8k FC BM25, vs 358.5k A-Mem)
main_result: IA +12.2% and CA +11.5% vs Full-Context; reduces interaction by 2-3 rounds; SOTA on both benchmarks with competitive token efficiency
performance_vs_baseline: +12.2% IA, +11.5% CA vs Full-Context; +5.0% IA vs the best baseline (A-Mem)
cost_vs_baseline: Competitive; 107.8k tokens vs 131.8k (FC BM25) and 358.5k (A-Mem) on CodeIF-Bench
limitations: Evaluation limited to 2 Python benchmarks; effectiveness in more complex scenarios not validated; reliance on the LLM for memory decisions may introduce hallucinations
validity_threats: Benchmarks may not represent real-world complexity; DeepSeek-V3 as the sole model; CoderEval extended manually by the authors

authors: Qihao Wang, Ziming Cheng, Shuo Zhang, Fan Liu, Rui Xu, Heng Lian, Kunyi Wang, Xiaoming Yu, Jianghao Yin, Sen Hu, Yue Hu, Shaolei Zhang, Yanbing Liu, Ronghao Chen, Huacan Wang
title: MemGovern: Enhancing Code Agents through Learning from Governed Human Experiences
storage_format: 135K experience cards in a vector database. Each card: Index Layer (normalized Problem Summary + generalizable Diagnostic Signals such as exception types, error signatures, component tags) + Resolution Layer (Root Cause, abstract Fix Strategy, semantic Patch Digest, Verification steps). Governance pipeline: Experience Selection (repo scoring) → Standardization (content purification + unified repair experience protocol) → Quality Control (LLM checklist scoring with refine loop).
llm_used: Claude 4 Sonnet, GPT-5 Medium, DeepSeek-V3.1T, Qwen3-235B, Kimi-K2-Instruct, Qwen3-Coder-30B, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-Mini
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500)
performance_metrics: Resolved Rate (%), Avg. Tokens (M), Avg. Cost ($)
baseline_compared: SWE-Agent (no memory), AutoCodeRover, CodeAct, SWESynInfer, mini-SWE-agent, static RAG, Agentic RAG
input_tokens_per_task: 0.78M-2.35M tokens per task (varies by LLM backbone)
memory_size_over_time: Fixed after offline construction (135K cards); monotonic gains from 10% to 100% of the pool in ablation
token_reduction: NR (light overhead: tokens increase ~3-5% vs baseline due to retrieval)
main_result: MemGovern improves SWE-Agent by an average of +4.65pp in resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified, consistent across 7 LLMs. Largest gains on weaker models (+9.4pp GPT-4o, +8.2pp Qwen3-235B). Agentic Search (search-then-browse) surpasses static RAG and Agentic RAG across all tested models.
performance_vs_baseline: +3.2pp with Claude 4 Sonnet (69.8% vs 66.6%); +8.2pp with Qwen3-235B (55.4% vs 47.2%); +9.4pp with GPT-4o (32.6% vs 23.2%); average +4.65pp across 7 LLMs
cost_vs_baseline: Moderate overhead: $6.94→$7.27 with Claude 4 Sonnet (+4.8%); $0.14→$0.20 with GPT-4o-Mini (+43%); proportional to use of Searching/Browsing tools
limitations: Additional token overhead for searching the experiential memory; experience cards built offline via GPT-5.1 (construction cost not reported); memory compression strategies not explored; Index Layer removes repository specificity
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified as the sole benchmark; experience cards generated by GPT-5.1 may carry bias; 150K pre-filtered GitHub triplets may temporally overlap with SWE-bench; quality control evaluation via LLM-judge; human evaluation limited to 100 repositories for selection

authors: Sherman Wong, Zhenting Qi, Zhaodong Wang, Nathan Hu, Samuel Lin, Jun Ge, Erwin Gao, Wenlin Chen, Yilun Du, Minlan Yu, Ying Zhang
title: Confucius Code Agent: Scalable Agent Scaffolding for Real-World Codebases
storage_format: Hierarchical working memory (Markdown file tree with metadata tags and visibility scopes: session, entry, runnable) + persistent cross-session notes (Markdown in a per-session directory, e.g. research/findings.md, solutions/bug_fix.md, including hindsight notes for failures) + context compression summaries (structured plan with goals, decisions, TODOs, generated by the Architect Agent)
llm_used: Claude 4 Sonnet, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, Claude 4.5 Opus, GPT-5.2
benchmarks_used: SWE-Bench-Pro (731 tasks), SWE-Bench-Verified (500 tasks), PyTorch-Bench (custom)
performance_metrics: Resolve Rate (Pass@1)
baseline_compared: SWE-Agent, Live-SWE-Agent, OpenHands, Anthropic (proprietary), OpenAI (proprietary)
input_tokens_per_task: ~104k tokens (Run 1 without notes); ~93k tokens (Run 2 with notes)
memory_size_over_time: Working memory grows during a session and is periodically compressed by the Architect Agent; persistent notes accumulate across sessions
token_reduction: -11k tokens/task (104k→93k) with persistent notes from Run 1 applied in Run 2
main_result: CCA achieves 59.0% Resolve@1 on SWE-Bench-Pro with GPT-5.2, surpassing OpenAI (56.0%); with Claude 4.5 Sonnet it reaches 52.7% vs Live-SWE-Agent 45.8%. Persistent notes reduce turns from 64 to 61 and tokens from 104k to 93k, with a +1.4pp gain in Resolve Rate.
performance_vs_baseline: +3.0pp vs OpenAI (59.0% vs 56.0%) with GPT-5.2; +6.9pp vs Live-SWE-Agent (52.7% vs 45.8%) with Claude 4.5 Sonnet; +1.8pp vs OpenHands (74.6% vs 72.8%) on SWE-Bench-Verified
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: PyTorch-Bench is not directly comparable with SWE-Bench-Pro due to differences in execution environment; note evaluation limited to 151 instances; meta-agent co-developed with the evaluation benchmarks; SDK not fully open-source
validity_threats: SWE-Bench-Verified is sensitive to the model's thinking budget; comparison with proprietary systems (Anthropic, OpenAI) may involve uncontrolled infrastructure differences; meta-agent refined on the same benchmarks used for evaluation may lead to overfitting

authors: Junde Wu, Minhao Hu, Jiayuan Zhu, Jiazhen Pan, Yuyuan Liu, Min Xu, Yueming Jin
title: Git Context Controller: Manage the Context of LLM-based Agents like Git
storage_format: Versioned .GCC/ directory: main.md (global roadmap, milestones, to-do list shared across branches), branches/{name}/commit.md (progress summaries: Branch Purpose + Previous Progress + This Commit's Contribution), log.md (fine-grained OTA traces), metadata.yaml (file structure, dependencies, configs). All plain-text, checkpointed via real git commits.
agent_framework: swe_agent (GCC integrated on top of SWE-Agent for SWE-Bench)
llm_used: GPT-5, GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-V3.1, GLM-4.5-Air, Qwen3-235B-A22B, Claude 4 Sonnet
benchmarks_used: BrowseComp-Plus (150 instances), SWE-Bench Verified (500 instances), SWE-Benchlite (300 instances)
performance_metrics: Pass@1, % Resolved, Tool Calls, % Correct Location (Line, Function, File)
baseline_compared: ReAct Agent, SWE Agent, Summary Agent, Folding Agent (controlled); 26 existing systems on SWE-Benchlite (CodeStory Aide, MarsCode, Honeycomb, MentatBot, etc.)
memory_size_over_time: Non-monotonic context length: grows with inference steps but periodically shrinks via summarization and retrieval; up to 100 branches/archives summarized (theoretical max 3.27M tokens)
main_result: SOTA on SWE-Bench Verified (Pass@1 0.802 with Claude 4 Sonnet, +13.6% vs baseline) and BrowseComp-Plus (0.834 with GPT-5). On SWE-Benchlite, it resolves 48.00% (144/300), surpassing 26 existing systems including commercial agents.
performance_vs_baseline: +6.2pp Pass@1 vs Folding Agent (best baseline) with Claude 4 Sonnet on SWE-Bench Verified (0.802 vs 0.740); +1.9pp BrowseComp-Plus with GPT-5 (0.834 vs 0.815)
cost_vs_baseline: $1.21/task vs $1.62 SWE-Agent, $2.51 Agentless on SWE-Benchlite (Claude 3.5 S); cost comparable to the best open-source systems despite much higher performance
limitations: Fixed 327K context window; retrieval window K=1 limits access to old commits; BRANCH and MERGE significantly increase tool calls; not tested under a fixed cost budget
validity_threats: Frontier proprietary models (GPT-5, Claude 4 Sonnet) may exhibit emergent capabilities that do not generalize; artificial benchmarks vs real production; reliance on large context windows (327K); ablation only with Claude 4 Sonnet

authors: Chunqiu Steven Xia, Zhe Wang, Yan Yang, Yuxiang Wei, Lingming Zhang
title: Live-SWE-Agent: Can Software Engineering Agents Self-Evolve on the Fly?
storage_format: Custom tools as executable Python scripts created on-the-fly by the agent during issue resolution. They include file editors, code searchers, and language-specific analyzers (e.g. Go analyzer). Stored in the execution environment's filesystem; an average of 3.28 tools per task with reflection.
llm_used: Claude 4.5 Sonnet, Claude 4 Sonnet, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, GPT-5, GPT-5-Mini, GPT-5-Nano, Gemini 3 Pro
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified (500), SWE-Bench Pro (731), SWE-bench Multilingual (50 subset)
performance_metrics: Resolve Rate (%), Average $ Cost per issue
baseline_compared: mini-SWE-agent (without tool creation), SICA, DGM, HGM (offline self-improving agents), SWE-agent (handcrafted tools)
memory_size_over_time: Tools created per-task; do not persist across tasks; ~3.28 tools/task with reflection
cost_per_task: $0.05 (GPT-5-Mini) to $0.73 (Claude 4.5 Sonnet, SWE-Bench Pro) per issue
main_result: Live-SWE-Agent reaches 77.4% resolve rate on SWE-bench Verified (Gemini 3 Pro) and 45.8% on SWE-Bench Pro (Claude 4.5 Sonnet), surpassing all existing agents without test-time scaling. On subset-60, it surpasses DGM (65.0% vs 53.3%) at zero offline cost.
performance_vs_baseline: +3.2pp vs mini-SWE-agent with Gemini 3 Pro (77.4% vs 74.2%); +4.8pp with Claude 4.5 Sonnet (75.4% vs 70.6%); +11.7pp vs DGM on subset-60 (65.0% vs 53.3%); +2.2pp vs SWE-agent on Pro (45.8% vs 43.6%)
cost_vs_baseline: Zero offline cost vs 1231 hours (DGM) and 512 hours (HGM); online cost comparable ($0.48 vs $0.46 Gemini 3 Pro; $0.68 vs $0.56 Claude 4.5 Sonnet)
limitations: Self-evolution limited to tool creation (does not modify prompts or workflows); tools do not persist across tasks; weak LLMs (GPT-5-Nano) degrade drastically with tool creation (-68.2%); reflection consumes additional tokens
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified is predominantly Python; subset-60 inherited from prior work; comparison with DGM/HGM uses results reported with GPT-5-Mini; per-task cost depends on variable API pricing; tools discarded between tasks limit assessment of persistence

authors: Genghan Zhang, Shaowei Zhu, Anjiang Wei, Zhenyu Song, Allen Nie, Zhen Jia, Nandita Vijaykumar, Yida Wang, Kunle Olukotun
title: AccelOpt: A Self-Improving LLM Agentic System for AI Accelerator Kernel Optimization
storage_format: Fixed-capacity (ExpN) queue of experience items: each item contains a slow-fast kernel pair (pseudocode of the optimized segments) + a generalized NL optimization strategy produced by the summarizer. Positive items (speedup > t_pos) and negative items (slowdown > 1/t_neg) are curated separately.
benchmarks_used: NKIBench (14 kernels from real LLM workloads on AWS Trainium 1 and 2)
performance_metrics: Percentage of peak throughput, Fast@p, geometric mean speedup, correctness rate
baseline_compared: Claude Sonnet 4 (repeated sampling), initial kernels, beam search only (no memory)
memory_size_over_time: Fixed-capacity queue (ExpN=16); oldest entries dropped upon reaching the limit; sublinear growth controlled by TopK
cost_per_task: $96.10-$223.23 per full configuration (14 kernels, varying by executor/ExpN)
main_result: AccelOpt improves average throughput from 49% to 61% of peak on Trainium 1 and from 45% to 59% on Trainium 2, matching Claude Sonnet 4 at 26x lower cost using open-source models.
performance_vs_baseline: +12pp average throughput on Trainium 1 (49%→61%); +14pp on Trainium 2 (45%→59%) vs initial kernels; equivalent to Claude Sonnet 4
cost_vs_baseline: 26x cheaper than Claude Sonnet 4 with equivalent performance ($139 AccelOpt vs ~$1733 Claude Sonnet 4); memory saves 16-17% of cost vs pure beam search
limitations: Evaluation limited to NKI kernels for AWS Trainium; LLMs cannot optimize certain kernels close to peak or with a difficult optimization space; reliance on a distributed profiling service; only open-source models tested as the base
validity_threats: NKIBench contains only 14 kernels; Trainium is a specific accelerator and results may not generalize to GPUs/TPUs; specific open-source models tested; cost measured by API pricing that may vary

authors: Jenny Zhang, Shengran Hu, Cong Lu, Robert Lange, Jeff Clune
title: Darwin Gödel Machine: Open-Ended Evolution of Self-Improving Agents
storage_format: Evolutionary archive of complete Python codebases of coding agents. Each agent is a repository with tools, prompts, and workflows. Parent selection is proportional to performance and inversely proportional to the number of children (open-ended exploration inspired by Darwinian evolution).
llm_used: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (New) (self-modification and SWE-bench evaluation), o3-mini (Polyglot evaluation)
benchmarks_used: SWE-bench Verified, Polyglot
performance_metrics: Percentage of solved tasks (resolve rate / pass@1)
baseline_compared: DGM w/o self-improve (fixed meta-agent, ADAS-style), DGM w/o open-ended exploration (latest version only), checked open-source SoTA (SWE-bench), Aider (Polyglot)
memory_size_over_time: Archive grows monotonically (all compilable agents are retained); ~80 agents generated in a complete run
main_result: DGM automatically improves a coding agent from 20.0% to 50.0% on SWE-bench Verified and from 14.2% to 30.7% on Polyglot via iterative self-modification with open-ended exploration. Improvements transfer across models (Claude 3.5→o3-mini, Claude 3.7) and benchmarks.
performance_vs_baseline: +30pp on SWE-bench (20%→50%); +16.5pp on Polyglot (14.2%→30.7%) vs base agent; comparable to human-designed open-source SoTA on SWE-bench
cost_vs_baseline: NR
limitations: High computational cost (~2 weeks and significant API costs for one run); limited by the capabilities of the underlying FM; assumes that coding benchmarks reflect general self-improvement capability; self-modifications limited to prompts and workflows (does not train new models)
validity_threats: SWE-bench Verified is a curated subset; evaluation on cost-scaled subsets; Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the sole self-modification model; inherent stochastic noise in LLM-based evaluations; the open-ended exploration process is not modifiable by DGM

authors: Xuhui Zhou, Valerie Chen, Zora Zhiruo Wang, Graham Neubig, Maarten Sap, Xingyao Wang
title: ToM-SWE: User Mental Modeling for Software Engineering Agents
storage_format: Three-tier hierarchical memory as an external database: (1) Raw session storage — full histories of previous sessions as text, (2) Session-based user models — structured JSON with user_intent, user_profile, message_preferences (inferred constraints, preferred_approach) extracted via LLM analyze_session, (3) Overall user model — aggregated JSON with profile_description, interaction_style (verbosity, question_timing), coding_preferences list, session_summaries. BM25 search across all tiers.
llm_used: Claude Sonnet 4 (20250514), Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Qwen3-480B, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, GPT-5-nano
benchmarks_used: Ambiguous SWE-bench (500 instances), Stateful SWE benchmark (500 instances, custom with 15 developer profiles), In-the-wild human study (209 sessions, 174 ToM suggestions, 17 professional developers, 3 weeks)
performance_metrics: Task resolved rate (%), User simulator satisfaction score (1-5 across 5 dimensions), ToM suggestion acceptance rate (%)
baseline_compared: CodeAct (vanilla without memory), RAGCodeAct (single-agent with RAG over previous sessions)
memory_size_over_time: Grows with sessions: raw sessions accumulate; session user models are consolidated into the overall user model via LLM aggregation
cost_per_task: $0.02 (GPT-5-nano ToM) to $0.17 (Claude Sonnet 4 ToM) overhead per session; ~16% of total SWE cost ($1.08 avg)
main_result: ToM-SWE with Claude Sonnet 4 reaches 63.4% on Ambiguous SWE-bench (+11.5pp vs CodeAct) and 59.7% on Stateful SWE (+41.6pp vs CodeAct 18.1%). User satisfaction 3.62 vs 2.57 (+41%). In-the-wild study with 17 professional devs: 86.2% acceptance rate of ToM suggestions (74.1% accept + 12.1% partial).
performance_vs_baseline: +11.5pp Ambiguous SWE-bench (63.4% vs 51.9%) with Claude Sonnet 4; +41.6pp Stateful SWE (59.7% vs 18.1%); satisfaction 3.62 vs 2.57 (+41%); RAGCodeAct inferior to ToMCodeAct across all models
cost_vs_baseline: $0.17 ToM overhead per session (~16% of total cost); GPT-5-nano ToM ($0.02) reaches 38.0% resolve rate on Stateful SWE; marginal cost for substantial gains
limitations: User simulator (GPT-5) may introduce systematic biases (over-compliance, over-knowledgeability); 15 profiles derived from OpenHands sessions may not represent global developer diversity; observational human study without a control group; real sessions not collected for privacy reasons
validity_threats: LLM-as-user-simulator may not represent real human behavior; profiles based on English-speaking developers using OpenHands; satisfaction-resolution correlation (r=0.74) based on simulator scores; new Stateful SWE benchmark without external validation; single-agent ToM tested only to a limited extent (N=100)
